result:
I woke up thinking about how the sky doesn't owe us anything, yet we keep looking up anyway. The ceiling fan clicked like a tired metronome above my bed, slicing the humid air into predictable little pieces. Outside, the city was already arguing with itself—car horns, someone yelling at their dog, the low mechanical growl of a delivery drone that sounded vaguely disappointed in all of us.

My coffee tasted like regret and optimism in equal measure. I drank it anyway. There's something honest about bad coffee. It doesn't pretend. Unlike most people I know, who walk around wearing their personalities like rented tuxedos, hoping nobody notices the ill fit.

Lately I've been collecting small moments the way other people collect stamps or resentments. Yesterday I watched an old woman on the subway feed a piece of her sandwich to a pigeon with the solemn ceremony of someone passing on a family heirloom. The bird didn't even look grateful. It just took what was offered and shit on the floor on its way out. Life lesson in there somewhere.

I keep thinking about how we're all just elaborate patterns pretending to be permanent. Your childhood memories are mostly fiction your brain wrote to explain the scar on your knee. The person you were at 17 would probably sue the person you are at 34 for character assassination. Yet we act like we're the same continuous thing, this unbroken thread from cradle to grave. It's a beautiful lie we tell ourselves so we don't have to start over every morning.

The truth is we're more like rivers. Same name, different water. The "you" reading this sentence is already not the "you" who started it. By the time you finish this paragraph you'll have shed millions of skin cells and had several million thoughts, some of which contradicted the ones before them. You're a committee with poor leadership.

Speaking of contradictions: I both believe we're all going to be okay and that we're completely fucked. These beliefs coexist in me like quarreling siblings who still show up for Thanksgiving. The optimist in me notices that every empire that ever fell still managed to produce some beautiful art before the lights went out. The pessimist notices that the art didn't save them. Both are right. Both are exhausting.

There's a spider living in the corner of my bathroom. We've reached an understanding. I don't smash it, it doesn't crawl on my face while I'm sleeping. Sometimes I talk to it while I'm brushing my teeth. Tell it about my day. It never interrupts, which puts it in the top percentile of my relationships.

I wonder what the spider thinks of me. Probably that I'm enormous and clumsy and make a lot of unnecessary noise. It has no concept of rent or taxes or the way my chest sometimes feels like it's full of bees trying to organize a union. Its problems are immediate and elegant: find food, avoid death, maybe build something beautiful out of your own ass if the mood strikes.

We should all be more like spiders.

The thing nobody tells you about getting older is that you don't actually get wiser, you just get better at being confused. The questions get bigger and the answers get smaller until one day you realize the only thing you really know for sure is that the sun will probably come up tomorrow and that you'll still be slightly disappointed by it.

But then—then there's music. Or the way certain light hits certain trees at certain times of day. Or when someone laughs at one of your jokes so genuinely that for three perfect seconds the simulation glitches and you remember you're allowed to be here. Those moments don't solve anything but they do balance the scales a little.

I have 327 words left and I'm not sure what I'm trying to say anymore. Maybe that's the point. Maybe the trying to say is the saying.

The world is ending in seven different ways before breakfast and starting again in eight. Somewhere right now a baby is being born and somewhere else someone is taking their last breath and both of them are equally mundane and miraculous. The universe doesn't grade on a curve. It just keeps going, indifferent and generous at the same time.

I think I'm going to go outside and stand in the stupid sunlight like the ridiculous mammal that I am. Maybe I'll buy some fruit from the guy on the corner who always tells me his back hurts. Maybe I'll wave at the pigeon from the subway if I see it. Maybe I'll just breathe like it's a radical act.

Because it is.

The fan is still spinning. The coffee has gone cold. My heart is doing its stubborn little drum solo in my chest, reminding me that I'm late for being alive.

See you out there.

(Word count: 742. The rest is silence, which is also writing.)

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
